items.q: Does a catalyst affect the position of equilibrium?
items.correct: No — it speeds up both the forward and reverse reactions equally, so equilibrium is reached faster but at the same position.
items.misconception: A catalyst shifts the equilibrium towards the products.
items.random_wrong: A catalyst increases the yield by being consumed in the forward reaction only.
items.why: Students associate 'catalyst' with 'helps the reaction' and assume that means 'pushes towards products' rather than 'speeds up both directions equally'.

items.correct: Particles spreading out from high to low concentration
items.why: Correct answer: Particles spreading out from high to low concentration. “Particles freezing in place” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In which state does diffusion happen fastest?
items.why: Correct answer: Gas. “Solid” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If diffusion takes 10 minutes at 20°C, at 50°C the same diffusion would be approximately how many times faster (it speeds up roughly)?
items.why: Exact answer: 2. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
